item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Came here from a video The Volgun made and man I really love this SCP. The exploration logs and journal by the soldier were both extremely well written and suspenseful. The fact that the journal and incident are both related is also really interesting.

+1

+300

Much love to everyone who voted and enjoyed this!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's actually scottish mythology, specifically from the Orkney islands! I'm aware of several inconsistencies in the logs, and Volguns recent video and the comments have brought to my attention some of the wording and dialogue is inconsistent with WWI era British slang. I've not had the time to really delve into correcting these issues (due to ongoing IRL stuff) but I'm going to make an effort to get to it hopefully sooner rather than later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Minor nitpick, but there’s an inconsistency in how you spell and punctuate “brass hat” on the July 14th & 15th, 1916 journal entry. Other than that, this article is wonderful and I gladly gave it an upvote. Irish mythology is the shit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This skip is in my top 10, maybe even top 5 articles. It's just so well written, it gives me the creeps the whole way through the article and the incident report. The journals improve it greatly, and you paint a great picture. Also, I'm pretty sure I understand what you're implying happens to people. I like it. Also,

Also, to clarify, they aren't being transported to an additional dimension. This isn't extra dimensional. Wherever the Nuckelavees come from, it's still on Earth.

For some reason, this makes it even scarier to me.

Anyway, really well done. Easy +1s.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This feedback is much more helpful, and contains points that I can use to better the piece. I appreciate the apology and will do my best to implement the necessary changes as soon as I'm physically able.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm sorry for my earlier comment, which was unconstructive and confrontational, but I'm going to clarify my position since I do think there are issues with the
execution of the journals that lead me to downvote. Let me be clear that I do not believe any of this suggests you have written anything in bad faith, or that any of
the problems I have are indicative of anything untoward on DrBleep's part.

+ Criticism
- Hide criticism
  • The journals still read to me like they have many forced Britishisms and don't read like realistic writing. Thinking on this some more, the principal problems I have are that the Britishisms are overloaded in the opening section and trip my internal 'performative fake Britishness' alarms, and some of them just seem inappropriate in context (e.g. calling a guy who you just saw murdered in front of you a 'wanker' right after calling an enemy combatant you killed in self defencea 'poor lad' seems completely out of place), or like they would make way more sense in speech than in writing after the fact (like reducing 'Before' and 'Except' to 'fore' and 'cept').
  • I don't buy the idea that the Nuckelavee would be well-known to Irish soldiers in WW1. It is an Orcadian legend unique to that specific context. The speculation that information about the Nuckelavee would be widespread enough in 1916 to justify it is, I think, dubious - it's not even a widespread piece of folklore now, and I suspect Irish people who know it now mostly know it from the internet and broader modern cultural osmosis driven by late-20th globalisation (especially given that, if your sample pool is 'Irish people who have read this article', they are predisposed to be internet users whose tastes would lead them towards information about grisly, obscure legends). It is possible that an Irish person would have heard of a Nuckelavee in 1916, yes, but the chances of a random pair of Irish recruits happening to be familiar enough with the legend to identify it in the way that they do, when none of the other soldiers are, is not credible to me.
  • Even if it were easily available in some form at that time, the way these narrative elements are presented has the unfortunate effect of inaccurately homogenizing Irish and Scottish culture (after all, someone who reads this article blind has no way of knowing, without further research, that Nuckelavees are not actually Irish). Presenting the Irishmen as the only ones in the know about a distinctly Scottish legend, while the Englishmen are all oblivious, has the effect of blurring Scottish and Irish culture together as an otherworldly Celtic 'other' complementing an English perspective, a trope with a long and unfortunate history. [This is what I meant in relation to the 'superstition' comment I made previously, rather than the crucifix.]
  • The previous issue is exacerbated by the brothers' role in the story being limited to the horror staple of demonstrating their fear, informing the English protagonist of the threat, and then dying horribly to demonstrate the Nuckelavee's power to him. I now know that you had more in mind for their characterisations than this, but it doesn't bleed through in the presentation, and they read as plot devices.

I hope this reconfigures my response into something which is actually helpful feedback rather than senseless sniping. Thanks for your patience in hearing me out a second time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a uh, pretty passionate response… I did a lot of work to make the logs not filled with British stereotypes, and on the first page of the comments I even worked with TL333s, who is also British to peel back the curtain and remove many of the Britishisms. I did quite a bit of research in my endeavor to attempt to capture the period appropriate language. I freely admit it may not be perfect, and am always willing to work to rectify those areas where I fall short.

The Northern Ireland is a glaring fault of my own, and a mistake I didn't catch at the time, or in any of the following feedback.

The last part of your comment is… upsetting on a somewhat personal level as I have strong Irish roots in my family. The backstory behind the Irish lads here has little to do with how superstitious they are, and more to do with how the horrors of war have pushed them into desperately clinging to a reminder of home in an attempt to survive what is occurring. Humans in general, cling to what is familiar in times of turmoil and change, and a crucifix handed to them by their parents, a detail about the item was never intended to be included in the article, so that the reader could interpret as needed, served as a memento of home.

As far as common knowledge, since the posting of this article, I've talked with a number of people from Ireland who knew about the Knuckelaveee, whether this was due to recent popular media, or the spread of folkloric myths has been difficult for me to say. There's a lot of projecting in your comment on to what my intentions were when I was writing, and while I'm definitely going to fix these issues, I wish they had been brought to my attention in a less confrontational manner.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: [EDIT: I have blanked this post, since it was overly rude and confrontational, poorly-judged, and unintentionally accusatory towards DrBleep, to whom I have apologized.]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What the Nuckelavees ran into SCP-106?

HELL ON EARTH!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As a fan of Norse, Celtic, and Orcadian mythology, I was happy to see the Nuckelavee get it's own entry on the mainlist. However, the entire scip feels a little too bland, almost like you just copied whatever traits made the Nuckelavee it's own myth without adding anything to it or giving your own twist/take on it for it to stand out.

Luckily, I wanted an SCP about the Nuckelavee and here it is.

You just got a close upvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huge shoutout to TheeSherm and LordStonefish for their awesome readthrough of my article. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CtJV-AWDv0M&feature=youtu.be

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting concept. I do appreciate articles that tend to give a twist on entities of myth, especially if it is one that I might have not known before. Also the WWI log and incident log did a really good job at showing how persistent and dreadful these entities could be.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's based around the Nuckelavee, a being from Orcadian mythology.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What thing(s) did you use as inspiration for this SCP? This one catches my eye purely because i've never heard of something like this before, and am curious to know if it is a completely original thing or if its based on some myth i just haven't heard before.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Orkney does involve the Scottish cultural area, but Nuckelavees are pretty well known in British Isles, so it would be a fairly common myth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This (warning for can be gross) can be more vivid graphic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I upvoted, but why the Irish guys? Doesn't Orkney belong to the Scottish cultural area?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is why we need to be able to upvote comments. XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a very interesting SCP. The creepy imaging for the horsemen is perfect for this. It fits in the universe without pushing other SCPs away and is just a solid page. I found problems in the journals though, the military theme with the Foundation going head to head with these things just didn't really work. This SCP gets a lot of its best properties from the mystery behind it, allowing it that people get away from it just makes it seem too ordinary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the skip, but not the supplement so much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wtf is kinky about Attack on Titan centaurs?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: thought it was a cool and kinky idea with great imagery, then look in comments and apparently find out it's a real (mythological) thing and I don't know

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And so the Horsemen break 100, riding deep into the night as they continue their rampage of destruction and abductions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see your points, and they are very good ones. I initially played off the trapped in the ocean bit, in a different way (where they could only go a certain distance from salt water bodies) but ended up scrapping that approach as it just ended up taking away from the "Harvester of human destruction" angle that the article ended up heading towards. With that said, I think it would be very plausible to work in an adaptation of them becoming enraged by smoke post contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the fact that Nuckelavees actually are basically trapped in the ocean during the summers, breath acidic breath/smoke, cause famine and plagues, and are enraged by smoke as I felt these aspects would be just…too over the top for an article.

The problem here is that all these aspects sound more entertaining than what's here, especially the "being trapped in the ocean" and "enraged by smoke" bits. What you did include is partly interesting (the twisted not-centaur form) but partly boring (not being able to cross fresh water, which is such a standard and silly trope that it's off-putting when not surrounded by even more interesting details).

Yes, including a laundry list of detail would be boring. Generally that's the fun part of an article adapting an established monster, though — finding interesting ways to get across their traits, as opposed to simply presenting them as-is. That's an issue with the article (for me) as it stands.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I appreciate your feedback. Oh what to do, this was a more difficult concept to work with than the articles I normally write, as this is more straight up old school monster horror. Arguably, that is the most difficult variety to write, and write well, but at the very least I think I did something right to earn a no vote from you.

With that said, I'd also like to say that while this is heavily influenced by the actual mythology, the only thing typical of Nuckelavees here are the pieces about avoiding freshwater, and and the general appearance. I altered a great deal of the mythos, including the fact that Nuckelavees actually are basically trapped in the ocean during the summers, breath acidic breath/smoke, cause famine and plagues, and are enraged by smoke as I felt these aspects would be just…too over the top for an article. Perhaps leaving some of that out hurt the article.

There were also things that I wanted to include, but ultimately ended up not doing so, based on feedback that the route I wanted to pursue would be too obvious. There are things that I can probably do to touch up, and solidify in the article proper once the contest is over, that I've considered doing, but I'm hesitant to do so without direction on where the weaker/more generic portions of the SCP are. Monster SCPs, as you've said, really have to be quite compelling these days, and I feel like I've done a fairly good job, but I could also do better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Creepy and well-written, but there isn't much here beyond "here's this monster from popular mythology".

I remember complaining about the exact same thing during the 1000 contest — some battles I won't win! :)

Generally, with monster SCPs like this, I tend to upvote them anyway if the logs compel me enough. This got close in some places, but ultimately fell short. Too much that felt generic.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was actually considering crosslinking to that one, I've received several comments about it. I also had a somewhat bigger plan for this article, something I don't want to spell out too much here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pretty straightforward use of the trope of mythical monsters feeding on the horrors of war, but competently done.

You might consider a crosslink with SCP-2368. Both Orcadian myths, both involve body horror and specifically human-animal hybrids, and both imply otherworlds.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved this….. I know it's an old monster from mythology, but it's nice to see a Foundation spin put on it. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nuckelavees were a thing before RWBY came along; I mean, it's part of Orcadian myths in RL.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah I was definitely feeling the inspiration towards the encounter at the end. I was just as surprised as you were that we didn't already have a Nuckelavees SCP but I'm glad you did it. You wrote the SCP well enough to separate it from RWBY while still maintaining all the core elements of the story and making it your own personal short horror story. All in all very good job my dude. Thanks for writing this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: RWBY definitely got me thinking about this concept, and you can obviously imagine my surprise when I discovered that we didn't already have Nuckelavees as a SCP. On the whole, I think RWBY inspired bits and pieces of this, but a lot of it was drawing upon aspects of the mythos themselves, and classic "Scary Monster" story lines to create something unique among the 3000 entries.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm feeling like this was very RWBY inspired, am I wrong? All in all pretty solid and good though. I was waiting for someone to make this and I'm glad someone finally did. Enjoyed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of scary stories I got told as a kid. Works very well for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just a heads up for everyone. I've added an expansive incident log to really flesh out the aspects of the concept you've yet to see in the WWI logs, and to provide some sort of penultimate hint at what is really going on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Imagery is good. It would really be supported by a suitably creepy picture. Given that you have a painting in the comments, perhaps use that and label it "a soldier's depiction of the entity"?

The concept is not particularly innovative. "Creature that makes people vanish" and "horseman of the apocalypse" are both common themes. This doesn't necessarily mean the entry is bad in itself, but it does place a higher burden on the execution to carry it.

Focusing on reports of an SCP from a certain period in history is a novel idea. It's good to have a different type of protagonist from the usual researchers, MTF teams and D-class. Showing it from the eyes of someone in the early 1900s helps construct that atmosphere of horror.

However, personally for me, the journals are a start, but they don't do enough. Perhaps because by the time the reader gets to them, we already know how the creature functions due to the extensive description. Perhaps it's also because they only give a glance into the monster and don't have a sting or payoff.

My suggestion would be to reduce the 'tell' of the monster and its abilities in the description and focus on the 'show' in the journals. You could either flesh out the existing plot of the Sommes soldier some more or you could include different accounts from other time periods.

For now, -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the first article in a while that cost me any sleep. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could have sworn I included that originally, went back and added a line about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The description doesn't mention anything about them being resistant or impervious to weaponry, even though the log mentions it shrugging off being shot at. The containment procedures imply it by saying all neutralization efforts have failed so far, but I think that part should go in the description.

I thought it was good enough to upvote though. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is some nice imagery in how you describe the horsemen here. Combined with the fact that they appear during times of strife, and strife is one thing humanity as whole is very good at, creates what feels like an old school monster skip to me.

I will agree that the log at some times gets a bit campy and awkward, but it does build up the tension and make me wonder how these horsemen made their moves during other wars and conflicts.

+1. Neat article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To clarify, he's not actually in the machinegun nest when most of the manifestations occur (Otherwise, hell yeah, he'd be shooting the hell out of that thing). However, this is a valid point…and I'll adjust it to reflect as much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One part about the log isn't very believable - if the victims are all soldiers, and the writer was manning a machine gun nest at one point when he saw these things up close, why doesn't he ever get the idea to, like, shoot at them? It's one of the first things anyone would think of, even if they're ordered to save bullets, one would still fire out of panic at least. It could be that bullets don't hurt them, but it doesn't say that he fired on them and it didn't work, it comes across as that he never tried at all. And that's just really hard to believe for jumpy soldiers who are armed 24/7 and see something threatening.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually want to clarify on a couple points you've made here. Harkand isn't dead, neither is Martin, or Brendan. None of them are dead, however I didn't want to outright state that within the article, so I put some very subtle details into the logs that may or may not have been easy to pick up on (some people have gotten it right away after reading, others haven't which is fine).

I juggled a delicate balance while writing this, I wanted to include an additional set of logs as I described in my response to Roget and the original discussion post, but based on the general feedback I received It felt more and more like an additional log would just take away from effect of the Journals. Perhaps once the 3000 contest has ended I will reevaluate this, and instead add an additional log straight in.

Also, to clarify, they aren't being transported to an additional dimension. This isn't extra dimensional. Wherever the Nuckelavees come from, it's still on Earth.

As an aside, is there anything you think I could do with the WWI logs that would help make it less anticlimactic?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only major complaint I have is that this feels like it ends just as it's getting started. Harkand's death feels decidedly abrupt and anticlimatic; that's not inherently a bad thing, but as the conclusion to your article it didn't work for me. I'm not all that curious about where they take people, simply because I don't have much of a reference point for that aspect of the horror. Stuff like 106's pocket dimension and the lake's ulterior dimension worked because we were provided certain context that allowed our minds to fill in the blanks; here, there's nothing, so it felt empty and kind of a useless plot point.

In more general terms, I do like the idea of a scipified Nuckalevee, but the execution and result ended up feeling unfortunately hollow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was afraid that the writing might come off that way. I've gone in and done a couple of things 1) I've removed/edited some of the more unrealistic writing things, like the one you mentioned above, and the "Am I a monster?" line, and attempted to adjust some of the others to be more realistic. 2) I've touched up the slang to be more era appropriate based on a bunch of digging and research I did, it's not perfect, and hopefully it doesn't make the logs more comedic, but I hope it resolves some of these issues.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not to be too crass, but it sounds like someone who isn't British trying to emulate the way a British person might have spoken back during WWI, and definitely does not come off as a document someone was actually writing. I mean, just starting with mentioning in real-time that an officer is coming; it'd take too long to write that to be able to react to the situation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm honestly not sure how to interpret the latter part of your statement, and might need a little bit of elaboration to understand where it's not working for you? I've received a lot of feedback regarding the Journals, mostly that they really really strongly make the article work well, but if there is something specific within them that doesn't really click for people, then I'd love to know what it is so I can improve it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Stands out from other Horsemen-related skips due to the creepy imagery and combination with Nuckelavees, but I couldn't grok the journals at all. The writing just… doesn't feel like writing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really and seriously thought about doing an additional log, specifically involving the Nuckelavees stalking a group of Foundation Agents in Fukishima following the Nuclear disaster, in fact that was what the original accompanying logs were going to be. That kind of fell to the wayside when I spent the entirety of today writing and editing the WWI logs, as it became really clear to everyone who read them that an additional log would hinder the impact of the piece in it's current form.

Fortunately for everyone reading, following the end of the contest I will be doing one, maybe two Tales in an incident log style format detailing the Fukishima incident.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The log was quite fun, I wish there was more to it but what was here gave me pause for thought.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Welp, here we go folks. This is my attempt for the 3000 contest, I hope you all enjoy.

I originally wanted to include an image…however I was unable to find anything realistic. I did find an Image I felt fit the mood of the article, but unfortunately it's a painting. I'll attach it below.

Many thanks to Shaggydredlocks, Psul, Tretter, Levi, ARD, Joreth, Roget, and so many others for help with developing and executing this concept!

The attached image is under Public Domain, and is a cropped/edit version of the original painting which can be found here.
